title: Jobs-to-be-Done Analysis
product_context: B2B SaaS project management platform for cross-functional teams (50-500 seat orgs)
source: 12 customer interviews, 47 NPS responses, 23 support tickets
author: JTBD Extractor

jobs.situation: preparing for a weekly leadership sync
jobs.action: see cross-team status at a glance
jobs.outcome: report progress without chasing 5 people for updates
jobs.evidence: "I spend 2 hours every Monday morning Slacking people for updates just to fill out a slide." — PM, fintech

jobs.action: instantly see downstream impact on dependencies
jobs.evidence: "By the time I realize something slipped, three other teams are already blocked." — Eng Lead, healthtech

jobs.action: reference how similar past projects actually went
jobs.outcome: give realistic estimates instead of guessing
jobs.evidence: "We keep making the same estimation mistakes because nobody looks back." — VP Eng, logistics

jobs.situation: onboarding a new team member mid-project
jobs.action: give them full context in one place
jobs.outcome: get them productive in days instead of weeks
jobs.evidence: "New hires spend their first two weeks just figuring out where things live." — PM, edtech

jobs.situation: a stakeholder asks about a specific deliverable
jobs.action: pull up its full history and current state
jobs.outcome: answer confidently without digging through Slack threads
jobs.evidence: "I scroll through 3 months of Slack to find who decided what and when." — PM, martech

jobs.outcome: advocate for my team without anxiety
jobs.evidence: "The worst feeling is when the CEO asks a question and I have to say 'let me get back to you.'" — Sr. PM

jobs.action: like nothing is falling through the cracks
jobs.outcome: sleep at night and not dread Monday mornings
jobs.evidence: "I wake up at 3am thinking 'did I follow up on that thing?' It's unsustainable." — PM, fintech

jobs.situation: dependencies slip and deadlines are at risk
jobs.outcome: resolve issues before they escalate into fire drills
jobs.evidence: "I don't mind problems — I mind finding out about them too late to do anything." — Eng Lead

jobs.action: someone who has everything under control
jobs.outcome: earn trust for larger, more strategic initiatives
jobs.evidence: "If I look like I'm scrambling, they won't give me the big projects." — Sr. PM

jobs.situation: working across engineering, design, and business teams
jobs.outcome: maintain trust when making hard tradeoff calls
jobs.evidence: "If people think I'm playing favorites with priorities, the whole system breaks down." — PM

jobs.outcome: get buy-in without being labeled 'the process person'
jobs.evidence: "The moment you say 'process improvement,' engineers tune out. I need data." — PM, logistics

translations.real_job: Know if I'm on track without manually compiling status
See dependency chains and identify cascade risks at a glance
Share progress with execs who won't log into our tool
Get alerted about risks in my existing workflow
Tell a compelling, data-backed story to justify my team's work
Connect project data to BI tools so leadership trusts the numbers
Stop reinventing the wheel — learn from what worked before
Find decision history for any deliverable without archaeology

recommendations: Prioritize auto-generated status intelligence — #1 opportunity and addresses the most emotionally charged pain point.
Build the dependency impact radar as a differentiator — nobody does proactive cascade alerting well.
Don't build a Gantt chart — the underlying job is dependency visibility, not the specific visualization.
Surface emotional jobs in marketing — 'Sleep better knowing nothing falls through the cracks' resonates more than 'Track tasks across teams.'

persona_comparison.confirms: "Overloaded PM" persona's core pain around status reporting is strongly validated. 11 of 12 interviewees mentioned this unprompted.
persona_comparison.expands: Emotional and social jobs were entirely missing from persona docs. Executive anxiety and desire for recognition are significant drivers.
persona_comparison.contradicts: "Engineering Lead" was assumed to care most about sprint velocity. Interviews reveal they care more about early warning on cross-team dependencies.
